title: Zistre - Scaling a Global Test-Prep Platform
summary: Co-founded and led product for a pre-employment test-prep platform serving 100K+ candidates across 62+ countries. I owned the three bets that turned global traffic into revenue - the right product, priced for every market, on a frictionless path to purchase.

sections.heading: What Zistre is, and what I did here
paragraphs: Zistre is a pre-employment test-prep platform that helps candidates and working professionals prepare for cognitive and behavioral assessments - CCAT, Wonderlic, Predictive Index, Watson Glaser, SHL, and personality tests - with GRE and TOEFL prep offered through a dedicated subdomain. Candidates rely on it to clear assessments used for promotions and as live interview rounds. To date it has served 100K+ candidates across 62+ countries.

As co-founder and Senior PM, I set product strategy and owned the growth and monetization roadmap end to end - under real startup constraints, with a lean team - collaborating directly with curriculum developers, engineering, and candidates.

facts.label: Assessments
facts.value: CCAT, Wonderlic, PI, Watson Glaser, SHL (GRE/TOEFL via subdomain)

sections.heading: Strong global demand. A funnel that leaked revenue.
paragraphs: The question that defined my work: how do we convert healthy global demand into revenue with a small team? Traffic was strong and evenly spread, but the defaults leaked money - a long one-month course few wanted, a single global price, a multi-step checkout, and a free-trial offer that quietly lost buyers.
I treated the funnel as the product and made three deliberate bets, sequencing them by where we were losing the most revenue first.

sections.heading: How I operated
paragraphs: I operated as both owner and builder: as co-founder I chose which bets were worth making; as Senior PM I instrumented each stage of the funnel, prioritized by highest leakage, and shipped with a lean engineering team. Every change was a measured bet, not a guess - I read behavior, acted, and held each decision to the data.

sections.heading: The right product
paragraphs: Our highest-converting product came from rejecting our first instinct. We launched with a comprehensive one-month course; uptake was weak. The insight that reshaped the business: candidates take these assessments on short notice - for a promotion or a live interview round - so they need to be ready in days, not weeks. I repackaged the entire offer around urgency.
list: Made a 4–7 day "emergency" prep course the flagship product, matched to how candidates actually buy → conversion +X%, revenue +X%
Added 1:1 mentorship as a paid upsell once candidates began requesting personalized help → higher-margin revenue + +X% AOV

sections.heading: Priced for every market
paragraphs: A single USD price was leaving money on the table across most of our footprint. Traffic split roughly evenly between developed and developing markets, but conversion in price-sensitive regions lagged far behind. I rebuilt pricing and payments around one truth: a buyer in Lagos and a buyer in London need different prices, currencies, and payment rails.
list: Market-based pricing - e.g., a course at $29 in developed markets sold at $19 in price-sensitive ones (India, Bangladesh, Pakistan, parts of Africa) → conversion there +X%
Currency localization for familiarity at the decision point - GBP, EUR, SGD, AUD, INR, USD → lower price friction, +X%

Re-architected the payment stack - Stripe for global coverage, Razorpay retained for South & Southeast Asia where its fees were lowest - replacing PayPal (poor forex, limited coverage) and Razorpay-everywhere (high failure rates outside the region) → payment success +X%
Abandoned-payment recovery - automated follow-ups with targeted discount/coupon codes to candidates who failed or dropped at the payment page → recovered X% of drop-offs

sections.heading: A frictionless path to purchase
paragraphs: Every step between intent and purchase was a place to lose a buyer, so I treated the funnel itself as the product - measuring each stage and removing whatever the data said was leaking, even when the fix was counterintuitive.
list: Collapsed a multi-page checkout into a single page after funnel data pinpointed drop-off at the intermediate course page → checkout drop-off ▼ X%

Removed the signup wall with automatic account creation at purchase - capturing only an email, provisioning the account, and emailing a temporary password to reset on first login → ▼ X% purchase friction

Removed the free tests via landing-page experimentation. Candidates were clicking "Free Test," deferring it, and never returning; cutting the free offer lifted conversion - counterintuitive, but the funnel was unambiguous → conversion +X%
Replaced that reassurance with a 30-day full-refund guarantee, so candidates bought with confidence instead of stalling → +X%
Added pre-purchase support - an AI chat assistant for in-the-moment questions and an optional free 15-minute session with an expert for a human walkthrough → +X% on purchase intent

decisions.title: Urgency over comprehensiveness (short course as flagship)
decisions.why: Matched real candidate timelines - they buy days before the test, not weeks.
decisions.tradeoff: Bet that depth-seeking buyers were the minority; validated by conversion.

decisions.title: Removed the free tests
decisions.why: Funnel data showed they delayed and lost buyers.
decisions.tradeoff: Gave up a lead-capture hook; mitigated with the 30-day refund.

decisions.title: Stripe + Razorpay split
decisions.why: Best global coverage with lowest regional fees.
decisions.tradeoff: Operational cost of two integrations; justified by recovered revenue.

sections.heading: What it added up to
extraParagraphs: The bets compounded: the right product brought the right buyers, market pricing and payments converted them globally, and a frictionless funnel captured the revenue.

reflection.heading: What I took from it
paragraphs: Founding and running product at once taught me to trust the funnel over instinct - removing a "free" perk to raise conversion is a call you only make by reading behavior honestly. With a lean team, the discipline wasn't doing everything; it was sequencing - fix the highest-leakage stage first, prove it with data, then move to the next. The compounding of those bets, not any one feature, is what scaled the business.
